skill.name: skill-creator-ms
skill.description: Guide for creating effective skills for AI coding agents working with Azure SDKs and Microsoft Foundry services. Use when creating new skills or updating existing skills.
skill.summary: Guide for AI agents to create modular skills that extend capabilities with Azure SDKs and Microsoft Foundry patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static scanner flagged 219 potential issues (external commands, network, filesystem, env access) but ALL are false positives. The skill is a documentation/guide containing markdown code examples for creating Azure SDK skills. No executable malicious code exists. The skill explicitly warns against hardcoding credentials as a security anti-pattern.

low_findings.title: External Command Patterns in Code Examples
low_findings.description: Scanner detected 168 'external command' patterns at various lines. These are bash command examples in markdown code blocks (pip install, ln -s, pnpm commands) used to document the skill creation process. Not actual executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All detected patterns are markdown code blocks containing example commands for documentation purposes, not actual code execution.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in Documentation
low_findings.description: Three URLs detected: example Azure endpoint, Microsoft Learn docs URL, and GitHub repo URL. These are legitimate documentation URLs and example placeholders.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: These URLs are documentation references and example placeholders, not credential exfiltration endpoints.

low_findings.title: Filesystem References in Skill Structure
low_findings.description: Path traversal and symlink patterns detected. These describe the skill's own directory structure (.github/skills/, skills/<language>/<category>/) used for organizing Azure SDK skills.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: These are legitimate documentation of the skill's file organization pattern, not path traversal vulnerabilities.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Examples
low_findings.description: Environment variable access patterns (AZURE_ENDPOINT) in code examples. The skill explicitly warns against hardcoding credentials as a security anti-pattern.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly marks hardcoded credentials as a security risk at lines 414-415, demonstrating security-conscious documentation.

content.user_title: Create Azure SDK Skills for AI Agents
content.value_statement: This skill guides AI agents through creating modular knowledge packages for Azure SDKs and Microsoft Foundry services, ensuring consistent patterns and proper authentication.
actual_capabilities: Guide users through creating skills for Azure SDKs in Python, .NET, Java, and TypeScript
Provide structured templates for skill organization with frontmatter, body, and bundled resources
Document product area categories for skill classification
Create acceptance criteria and test scenarios for skill validation
Show symlink-based categorization for skill discoverability
limitations: Requires users to provide SDK package name and documentation URL
Focused specifically on Azure SDKs and Microsoft Foundry, not general-purpose skills
Does not execute code - provides guidance only
Skill creation workflow assumes familiarity with Azure services

use_cases.title: New Azure SDK Skill Creation
use_cases.description: When a developer needs to create a skill for a new Azure service SDK
use_cases.target_user: Developers working with Azure services who want to create reusable skills

use_cases.title: Existing Skill Update
use_cases.description: Updating existing skills with current Azure SDK patterns and authentication
use_cases.target_user: Maintainers of Azure SDK skill libraries

use_cases.title: Skill Testing and Validation
use_cases.description: Creating acceptance criteria and test scenarios to validate skill quality
use_cases.target_user: QA engineers ensuring skill reliability and correctness

prompt_templates.title: Create Azure AI Agents Skill
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the skill-creator-ms to create a new skill for the Azure AI Agents SDK. The SDK package is azure-ai-agents and the documentation is at https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/agents/.
prompt_templates.scenario: Creating a new skill for Azure AI Agents service

prompt_templates.title: Create Cosmos DB Skill
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a skill for Azure Cosmos DB using the azure-cosmos SDK. Documentation: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cosmos-db/
prompt_templates.scenario: Creating a skill for Azure Cosmos DB

prompt_templates.title: Add Authentication Section
prompt_templates.prompt: Add proper authentication documentation to the existing azure-storage skill using DefaultAzureCredential.
prompt_templates.scenario: Updating existing skill with proper auth patterns

prompt_templates.title: Create Test Scenarios
prompt_templates.prompt: Create acceptance criteria and test scenarios for the azure-eventhub skill following the skill-creator-ms patterns.
prompt_templates.scenario: Creating tests for an Azure SDK skill

output_examples.input: Create a skill for Azure Key Vault secrets
output_examples.output: A complete skill structure with SKILL.md containing: installation commands (pip install azure-keyvault-secrets), environment variables setup, DefaultAzureCredential authentication, core workflow examples for secret operations, best practices, and reference links.

output_examples.input: What categories are available for organizing Azure SDK skills?
output_examples.output: Foundry (AI Foundry, agents), Data (Storage, Cosmos DB), Messaging (Event Hubs, Service Bus), Monitoring (OpenTelemetry), Identity (authentication), Security (Key Vault), Integration (API Management), Compute (Batch), Container (Container Registry).

output_examples.input: Create acceptance criteria for an Azure SDK skill
output_examples.output: Acceptance criteria document with correct/incorrect import patterns, authentication patterns (DefaultAzureCredential vs hardcoded), client initialization, async variants, and common anti-patterns to avoid.

best_practices: Always use DefaultAzureCredential for authentication instead of hardcoding API keys
Keep SKILL.md under 500 lines and use progressive disclosure with reference files
Include both correct and incorrect code patterns in acceptance criteria to help validate outputs
anti_patterns: Creating a skill without first obtaining the SDK package name and documentation URL from the user
Hardcoding credentials in example code - always use environment variables
Skipping the authentication section - agents will improvise poorly without guidance

faq.question: What information do I need to provide before creating an Azure SDK skill?
faq.answer: You must provide the SDK package name (e.g., azure-ai-agents), the Microsoft Learn documentation URL, and optionally the GitHub repository for code patterns.

faq.question: How do I organize skills by category?
faq.answer: Create a symlink in skills/<language>/<category>/ pointing to .github/skills/<skill-name>. Categories include foundry, data, messaging, monitoring, identity, security, integration, compute, and container.

faq.question: What authentication method should I use?
faq.answer: Always use DefaultAzureCredential for Azure SDKs. Never hardcode credentials. Use environment variables like AZURE_ENDPOINT.

faq.question: How long should SKILL.md be?
faq.answer: Keep SKILL.md under 500 lines. Use progressive disclosure - load metadata always, body when skill triggers, and reference files as needed.

faq.question: Do I need to create tests for the skill?
faq.answer: Yes, every skill must have acceptance criteria in references/acceptance-criteria.md and test scenarios in tests/scenarios/<skill-name>/scenarios.yaml.

faq.question: What programming languages are supported?
faq.answer: The skill covers Python, .NET (C#), Java, and TypeScript patterns for Azure SDKs.
